tactics.name: Cost of Inaction
tactics.structure: Status quo pain → What it costs to do nothing → The alternative → CTA
tactics.guidance: Show the price of staying put. Use real numbers — '$800/year in emergency vet bills' not 'it costs more than you think'. The maths must be specific and verifiable.

tactics.name: Problem-Agitate-Solve
tactics.structure: Name the pain → Twist the knife (make it visceral) → Present relief → CTA
tactics.guidance: The agitation step is where most ads fail — they name the problem but don't make it hurt. Use sensory detail, specific moments, or surprising consequences.

tactics.name: Before-After-Bridge
tactics.structure: Paint the 'before' state → Show the 'after' state → Bridge = your product → CTA
tactics.guidance: The 'before' must be recognisable — the reader sees themselves in it. The 'after' must be specific and desirable. The bridge is short: one sentence connecting the two.

tactics.name: StoryBrand
tactics.structure: Customer is the hero → Brand is the guide → Name the villain (problem) → Show the plan → Call to action → Show success → Warn of failure
tactics.guidance: The customer is ALWAYS the hero, never the brand. The brand is the guide with a plan. Works best in longer formats (email, LP) where you have room for the full arc.

tactics.name: AIDA
tactics.structure: Attention (hook) → Interest (expand on the hook) → Desire (show the benefit) → Action (CTA)
tactics.guidance: Classic funnel structure. Each step earns the next. Don't rush to the CTA — interest and desire are where the work happens.

tactics.name: Social Proof Stack
tactics.structure: Lead with proof (testimonial/stat/milestone) → Stack more proof → Connect to reader's situation → CTA
tactics.guidance: Stack at least 3 proof points. Mix types: a quote, a number, a named person. The reader should feel like they're the last one who hasn't acted.

tactics.name: Comparison / Us vs Them
tactics.structure: Name the alternative (category, not competitor) → Show the gap → Prove your advantage → CTA
tactics.guidance: Compare against the CATEGORY default, never a named competitor. 'Traditional pet insurance charges $180/month and rejects 40% of claims' — not 'Brand X charges...'.

tactics.name: Founder Story
tactics.structure: Founder's personal moment → The problem they saw → Why they built this → What it means for the reader → CTA
tactics.guidance: The founder's story is only valuable if it connects to the reader's problem. Start with the moment that triggered everything. Be specific — date, place, feeling.

tactics.name: Direct Offer
tactics.structure: State the offer → List what's included → Remove risk → CTA
tactics.guidance: No story, no emotion — just the deal. This works for warm audiences who already know the brand. Specificity is everything: exact price, exact inclusions, exact risk removal.

tactics.name: Curiosity Gap
tactics.structure: Reveal a surprising fact → Create an incomplete picture → Promise the resolution → CTA
tactics.guidance: The gap must be genuinely interesting, not clickbait. 'We tested 14 brands...' works because the reader wants to know which 2 passed. The resolution must be real.

tactics.name: Risk Reversal
tactics.structure: Name the fear → Acknowledge it → Remove the risk (guarantee, trial, refund) → Prove it works → CTA
tactics.guidance: The risk reversal must be specific and concrete — 'cancel anytime, no questions' not 'satisfaction guaranteed'. Address the actual objection the audience has.

tactics.name: Before / After
tactics.structure: Show the 'before' (with specifics) → Show the 'after' (with specifics) → Explain the difference → CTA
tactics.guidance: Both before and after need concrete details — dollars, time, feelings. '$1,300/year in emergency bills' → '$50/month, everything covered'. The contrast does the selling.

tactics.name: Educational / How It Works
tactics.structure: Teach something the reader didn't know → Connect it to their situation → Show how you solve it → CTA
tactics.guidance: The teaching moment must be genuinely useful — not a disguised sales pitch. The reader should learn something real even if they never buy. Connect the lesson to your product naturally.
